- Each index is optimized for the morphological features of its language (plurals, possessives, etc.), for both documents and queries.

Each index contains all documents available on CanLII, across all languages. If CanLII has official translations, the index includes only the translation corresponding to the index's language.

05. 5.













    [Adga **Group** Consultants Inc. v. Lane](https://www.canlii.org/en/on/onscdc/doc/2008/2008canlii39605/2008canlii39605.html?resultId=2d3dca050e054c9faed5f19233b709de&searchId=2025-08-11T22:33:09:342/99647145e16b4cd0abeb3a8b59fcf3ee&searchUrlHash=AAAAAQAFZ3JvdXAAAAAAAQ), 2008 CanLII 39605 (ON SCDC)

    cited [536 times](https://www.canlii.org/en/#search/origin1=/en/on/onscdc/doc/2008/2008canlii39605/2008canlii39605.html)







    Divisional Court — Ontario



    2008-08-08  \|  63 pages  \|  cited by [536 documents](https://www.canlii.org/en/#search/origin1=/en/on/onscdc/doc/2008/2008canlii39605/2008canlii39605.html)



    AI-generated

    The Tribunal upheld findings that an employer discriminated against an employee with bipolar disorder by terminating him without fulfilling procedural and substantive accommodation duties. The employer's actions, including failure to explore reasonable accommodations, were deemed unreasonable, and damages were awarded for discrimination and mental anguish.



    DamagesRights and freedoms





    **Labour and employment** — Disability discrimination — Termination of employment — Employer dismissed complainant ten days after hiring upon disclosure of bipolar disorder — Tribunal found termination was discriminatory under Human Rights Code — Did the employer discriminate against the complainant on the basis of disability? — Human Rights Code, R.S.O. 1990, c. H.19, s. 5 — Employers must not terminate employment based on disability without meeting accommodation obligations



    **Workplace health and safety** — Duty to accommodate — Procedural and substantive duties — Employer failed to explore accommodation options for employee with bipolar disorder — Tribunal found employer did not meet procedural or substantive duty to accommodate short of undue hardship — Did the employer fail to accommodate the complainant’s disability? — Employers must engage in meaningful accommodation efforts before termination



    **Workplace health and safety** — Remedies — Damages and public interest orders — Tribunal awarded $35,000 in general damages, $10,000 for mental anguish, and public interest remedies — Were the damages and remedies reasonable and within the Tribunal’s authority? — Human Rights Code, R.S.O. 1990, c. H.19, s. 41(1) — Tribunals may award damages for discrimination and impose public interest remedies to ensure compliance



    **Administrative law** — Expert evidence — Tribunal qualified non-medical expert and psychiatrist to testify on bipolar disorder and workplace stigma — Employer challenged qualifications and relevance of expert evidence — Was the Tribunal’s qualification and reliance on expert evidence reasonable? — Tribunals have discretion to admit expert evidence if relevant and necessary to understanding the issues



    **Rights and freedoms** — Comparator group analysis — Disability discrimination — Tribunal held comparator group analysis unnecessary in disability-based termination cases — Employer argued comparator group was required to establish discrimination — Is comparator group analysis necessary in disability-based employment discrimination? — Disability cases focus on individualized accommodation rather than comparator group analysis







    \[…\]   ADGA **Group** Consultants Inc. v. Lane et al. \[Indexed as: ADGA **Group** Consultants Inc. v. Lane\]   \[…\]  Comparator **group** \-\- Appellant's submissions \[77\] ADGA submits that the decision of the Tribunal must fall because Lane and the Commission did not establish a correct \[page669\] comparator **group** \-\- indeed any comparator **group** against which the treatment of Lane could be measured.   \[…\]   from treating employees with disabilities differently from other employees within the same comparator **group**. Therefore, the Tribunal could only make a finding of discrimination if there was evidence that the complainant was treated differently than other employees in a particular comparator **group** because of his disability.   \[…\]

    AI-generated

    The Court held that appeals from masters should follow the same deferential standard of review as appeals from judges, requiring palpable and overriding error or legal misapprehension to interfere with findings. The motions judge erred by treating the appeal as a de novo hearing and substituting their discretion. Appeal allowed.



    Practice and procedure





    **Civil procedure** — Appeals from master’s decisions — Standard of review — Whether the same standard of review applies to appeals from masters and judges — Motions judge erred in treating appeal as a de novo hearing — Standard of review for findings of fact and discretion is deference unless there is a palpable and overriding error — Correctness standard applies to errors of law — Housen v. Nikolaisen framework applied



    **Evidence** — Security for costs — Plaintiffs failed to demonstrate impecuniosity — No evidence adduced to support merits of claim — Master’s finding that impecuniosity was not shown entitled to deference — Closer scrutiny of merits warranted where impecuniosity is not established — Plaintiffs failed to show claim had a reasonable chance of success — Rule 56.01 of the Rules of Civil Procedure applied

(3d) 131   \[…\]  \[1\] LOW J.: -- This is an appeal by The Economical Insurance **Group** from the order of Pitt J. dated February 12, 2007 \[reported at \[2007\] O.J. No. 473, 40 C.P.C. (6th) 195 (S.C.J.) \] setting aside the order of Case Management Master Abrams which required the respondents, the plaintiffs, to post $45,000 in security for costs.   \[…\]

09. 9.













    [Dumbrell v. The Regional **Group** of Companies Inc.](https://www.canlii.org/en/on/onca/doc/2007/2007onca59/2007onca59.html?resultId=9111d52032b944fbb39aaaa4c16c1d72&searchId=2025-08-11T22:33:09:342/99647145e16b4cd0abeb3a8b59fcf3ee&searchUrlHash=AAAAAQAFZ3JvdXAAAAAAAQ), 2007 ONCA 59

    cited [328 times](https://www.canlii.org/en/#search/origin1=/en/on/onca/doc/2007/2007onca59/2007onca59.html)







    Court of Appeal for Ontario — Ontario



    2007-01-31  \|  35 pages  \|  cited by [328 documents](https://www.canlii.org/en/#search/origin1=/en/on/onca/doc/2007/2007onca59/2007onca59.html)



    AI-generated

    An employee was entitled to 50% of profits from a project initiated during employment, even if profits were realized post-termination. The court limited liability to profits earned by the employer and its controlled entities, excluding third-party investors. The employer's directing mind was not personally liable.



    ContractsInterpretationLabour and employment





    **Contracts** — Breach of contract — Employment contract — Employee entitled to 50% of profits from projects brought to employer — Profits earned after termination of employment — Did the trial judge err in holding that the employee was entitled to post-termination profits? — Contractual interpretation focused on objective meaning of terms and context — Employee entitled to profits earned after termination if tied to work performed during employment



    **Contracts** — Breach of contract — Damages — Third-party investors — Trial judge awarded employee 50% of profits earned by third-party investors — Did the trial judge err in awarding profits not earned by the corporate employer? — Profits earned by bona fide third-party investors excluded from employee’s entitlement — Employee entitled only to profits earned by corporate employer or entities controlled by it



    **Labour and employment** — Personal liability — Corporate veil — President and directing mind of corporate employer held personally liable for breach of contract — Did the trial judge err in holding the president personally liable? — No basis for piercing the corporate veil or finding personal liability — Contract was between employee and corporate employer only — No evidence of inducement of breach by president







    \[…\]   Dumbrell v. The Regional **Group** of Companies Inc. et al. \[Indexed as: Dumbrell v. The Regional **Group** of Companies   \[…\]  \[1\] The respondent, J. Michael B. Dumbrell ("Dumbrell"), was employed by the appellant, the Regional **Group** of Companies Inc. ("Regional"), for about one year beginning in November 1998.   \[…\]   \[9\] Regional would sometimes put together **groups** of investors or syndicates to purchase and develop properties. The properties would be located by Regional and purchased in trust by a shell company for the investors.   \[…\]\

    AI-generated\
\
    The Court held that ambiguous contract terms allow for extrinsic evidence of surrounding circumstances to aid interpretation. It overturned a summary judgment, finding a genuine issue for trial regarding the termination clause's meaning and emphasizing that the legal burden to prove no genuine issue remains on the moving party.\
\
\
\
    ContractsInterpretationPractice and procedure\
\
\
\
\
\
    **Contracts** — Termination provisions — Interpretation — Ambiguity — Agreement between parties provided for automatic renewal subject to 120 days' prior written notice — Whether termination provision was ambiguous and reasonably susceptible to more than one meaning — Proper interpretation of termination clause in light of surrounding circumstances — Evidence of surrounding circumstances admissible to interpret ambiguous contract terms — Governing principles of contract interpretation applied\
\
\
\
    **Contracts** — Extrinsic evidence — Parol evidence rule — Whether extrinsic evidence of surrounding circumstances is admissible to interpret a contract — Supreme Court of Canada’s decision in Eli Lilly & Co. v. Novopharm Ltd. distinguished — Words in a contract take meaning from their context — Admissibility of evidence to resolve ambiguity in contract terms\
\
\
\
    **Civil procedure** — Summary judgment — Genuine issue for trial — Legal and persuasive burden on moving party to show no genuine issue for trial — Evidentiary burden on responding party — Failure to tender evidence by responding party not automatically resulting in summary judgment — Governing principles from Guarantee Co. of North America v. Gordon Capital Corp. applied\
\
\
\
    **Civil procedure** — Partial summary judgment — Appropriateness of granting partial summary judgment in the circumstances — Whether motions judge erred in dismissing part of the plaintiff’s claim while other claims remained unresolved — Proper application of Rule 20 of the Rules of Civil Procedure\
\
\
\

    A motorist injured in a drive-by shooting was denied accident benefits as the injuries were not directly caused by vehicle use. The Court upheld the stringent causation requirement under the 1996 Schedule, finding the shooting an intervening act breaking the chain of causation. Appeal dismissed.\

\
    Insurance\
\
\
\
\
\
    **Insurance** — Statutory accident benefits — Definition of "accident" — Plaintiff rendered paraplegic in a drive-by shooting while driving — Whether the use or operation of an automobile directly caused the injuries — Statutory Accident Benefits Schedule, O. Reg. 403/96, s. 2(1) — Direct causation requirement excludes injuries caused by intervening acts such as gunshots\
\
\
\
    **Insurance** — Causation — "But for" test — Whether the "but for" test satisfies the direct causation requirement under the 1996 Schedule — "But for" test screens out irrelevant causes but does not establish legal causation — Legal causation requires an unbroken chain of events without independent intervening forces\
\
\
\
    **Insurance** — Interpretation of "accident" — Applicability of the Amos test — Amos test no longer applies under the 1996 Schedule due to the stricter "directly causes" requirement — Nexus or causal relationship insufficient where injuries result from random acts like a drive-by shooting\
\
\
\
    **Insurance** — Legislative gap — Removal of "indirectly" from the definition of "accident" — Alleged inconsistency between the Insurance Act and the 1996 Schedule — Court held that the gap is statutorily mandated — Legislative intent to limit coverage to injuries directly caused by the use or operation of an automobile\
\
\
\
\
\

    **Civil procedure** — Appeals — Standard of review — Appeal from master’s decision — No principled reason to distinguish between standard of review for master and judge — Decision of master interfered with only for error of law, wrong principles, or palpable and overriding error — Did the motion judge err in treating the appeal as a hearing de novo? — Standard of review principles from Housen v. Nikolaisen applied\

    **Civil procedure** — Costs — Security for costs — Rule 56.01 of the Rules of Civil Procedure — Master’s reference to prima facie entitlement to security for costs based on plaintiffs’ non-residency a misstatement — Did the master err in granting security for costs? — Master’s reasons as a whole upheld — No reversal of onus or unreasonable result\
\
\
\
    **Civil procedure** — Appeals — Motion judge’s role — Motion judge substituted own view of evidence on impecuniosity and merits of claim — Did the motion judge err in setting aside the master’s order? — Motion judge erred in law by treating appeal as a hearing de novo — Master’s findings on evidence and principles upheld\

O.28, s. 36\
\

P.13, s. 38\

    **Statutory interpretation** — Standard of review — Interpretation of arbitration clauses — Whether the application judge’s interpretation of the arbitration clause involved a question of law or mixed fact and law — Determination of the applicable standard of review — Reviewability of contractual interpretation decisions under the correctness or palpable and overriding error standards\
\
\
\
    **Contracts** — Waiver provisions — Arbitration clauses — Failure to file a notice of arbitration with the Institute within 12 months — Whether failure to file constitutes an irrevocable waiver of the claim — Interpretation of “file” in the context of the waiver provision — Application of the Arbitration Act, 1991 and the Institute’s rules\
\
\
\
    **Civil procedure** — Final or interlocutory orders — Arbitration-related applications — Whether the application judge’s decision interpreting the arbitration clause constituted a final order or an interlocutory order — Determination of the nature of the order based on its effect on the proceeding — Principles governing the finality of orders\
\

metadata.description: CanLII is a non-profit organization managed by the Federation of Law Societies of Canada. CanLII's goal is to make Canadian law accessible for free on the Internet. This website provides access to court judgments, tribunal decisions, statutes and regulations from all Canadian jurisdictions.
